coredata.dc:title: Controlled synthesis and superior UV-visible photocatalytic activity of carbon quantum dots encapsulated with silica
coredata.dc:creator: Aziz, Hamid
Saeed, Aamer
Akhtar, Javeed
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we report a facile one-step approach to prepare carbon quantum dots encapsulated with silica. Carbon quantum dots (C-QDs) were synthesized using Glucose as precursor and sulfuric acid (98%) as carbonating agent in aqueous medium. The encapsulation of C-QCDs was then achieved by in situ hydrolysis of precursor Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) through Solgel method. The structural studies of C-QDs doped silica was done by powdered X-ray diffraction (p-XRD). The optical characterization of the as-prepared C-QDs was performed by Ultraviolet-visible (UV-vis) spectroscopy. Consequently, the as-prepared C-QCDs were found to be highly fluorescent with little crystallinity. The morphological investigation of as-made silica and silica encapsulated C-QCDs was studied by field emission scanning electron (FE-SEM). In order to study photodegradation studies methylene blue was used as model compound. C-QCDs encapsulated with silica showed higher degradation (98.5%) in comparison to the pristine C-QDs (38%) and silica (76%) in neutral media.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Methylene blue
Photocatalysts
Sol-gel silica
Carbonating agent

Field Emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) analysis
Comparative photocatalytic degradation of MB dye
Effect of changing concentration of photocatalysts and MB dye

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a-b). p-XRD spectrum of the as-prepared (a) C-QDs, (b) doped silica, (c) C-QDs doped silica, (b) C-QDs doped calcined silica.
UV-visible spectrum of the as-prepared C-QDs
(a-d). FSEM images of (a-b) silica (c-d) doped silica at different resolutions
(a-c) UV-Visible degradation of MB with (a). C-QDs, (b). silica, (c). doped silica
(a-b). UV-Visible degradation of MB (a). Increased C-QDs concentration, (b). Dilution
(a-b). MB degradation in (a) acidic medium (b) basic medium
Photocatalytic degradation of MB dye in calcined doped silica
(a-b). Reproducibility of the doped photocatalysts (a). Used doped calcined silica (c). Re-used doped calcined silica

Synthetic pathway adopted for the synthesis of as-prepared C-QDs [37]

head.ce:title: Controlled synthesis and superior UV-visible photocatalytic activity of carbon quantum dots encapsulated with silica

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Quaid-i-Azam University, Islamabad 45320, Pakistan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Mirpur University of Science and Technology (MUST)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cFunctional nanomaterials Lab (FNL), Department of Chemistry, Mirpur University of Science and Technology (MUST), 10250 (AJK) Mirpur, Pakistan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we report a facile one-step approach to prepare carbon quantum dots encapsulated with silica. Carbon quantum dots (C-QDs) were synthesized using Glucose as precursor and sulfuric acid (98%) as carbonating agent in aqueous medium. The encapsulation of C-QCDs was then achieved by in situ hydrolysis of precursor Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) through Solgel method. The structural studies of C-QDs doped silica was done by powdered X-ray diffraction (p-XRD). The optical characterization of the as-prepared C-QDs was performed by Ultraviolet-visible (UV-vis) spectroscopy. Consequently, the as-prepared C-QCDs were found to be highly fluorescent with little crystallinity. The morphological investigation of as-made silica and silica encapsulated C-QCDs was studied by field emission scanning electron (FE-SEM). In order to study photodegradation studies methylene blue was used as model compound. C-QCDs encapsulated with silica showed higher degradation (98.5%) in comparison to the pristine C-QDs (38%) and silica (76%) in neutral media.

ce:list-item.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

entry.italic: Inorganic Chemistry, Materials Chemistry, Organic Chemistry, Physical Chemistry
Methylene Blue, Carbon Quantum Dots, Silica

Solgel synthesis of C-QDs encapsulated silica is reported. Their characterizations involve p-XRD, UV-Visible, and FSEM. Finally, the photocatalytic degradation of MB dye was carried out under UV-Visible irradiation.
The data is available with this article.

ce:section.ce:para: Industrialization has caused severe environmental as well as health risks due to the discharge of toxic effluents into the aqueous environment [1]. Industrial water contaminated with various toxic chemicals can easily become part of drinking water [2,3]. Modern textile industries constitute main source of aqueous pollution by utilizing highly stable and carcinogenic organic dyes like Victoria Blue, Caramine, and Thymol B [4]. Major portion of these dyes comprises nonbiodegradable, toxic, and colored pigments that are released in wastewater streams. Similarly, biomedical laboratories utilize organic dyes like Methylene blue (MB) for staining purposes. Such harmful dyes have severe side effects when encounter with living tissues. Hence, it is necessary to reduce their toxicity within permissible limits. Therefore, strategies like ozonation, flocculation, neutralization, and chemical reductions have been adopted to overcome this challenge. However, such reduction reactions are very slow and time-consuming [1,5-7]. Hence, nanocatalysts like quantum dots have been employed to accelerate such reactions owing to their higher surface energy and excellent photo-induced electron transfer properties [8,9].

Carbon quantum dots (C-QDs) are quasi-spherical green nanomaterials from Carbon family. They display photoluminescence properties comparable to the related semiconductor quantum dots [10]. C-QDs outperform their counterparts because of their unusual physico-chemical properties including aqueous miscibility, surface functionalization, and stability [11]. Furthermore, photoinduced electron transfer properties impart them application in catalysis, sensors, and optoelectronic devices [12,13]. Electron donor or acceptor molecules quench photo-excited C-QDs for solar energy conversion [14,15]. Their near-infrared emissions and superior up-conversion photoluminescence efficiencies make them ideal candidates in designing complex photocatalytic systems for full-spectrum sunlight utilization [16–20]. Reports show that surface defects and composition both control catalytic efficiency of C-QDs. The excitons either recombine and emit photons or become the source of electron-hole pair [21]. The synthesis of C-QDs composites with plasmonic, chalcogenide, and inorganic nanoparticles (ZnO, SiO2) have been reported with enhanced physicochemical properties, and up-conversion photoluminescence efficiencies which turn them efficient photocatalysts [22–25]. Mendes et al. employed N-doped C-QDs/Titania composites as photocatalysts for MB degradation under UV-Visible irradiation [26]. Titania (TiO2) is an attractive photocatalyst, but the associated wide band gap reduces its activity [27]. Hence, Wang et al. employed the surficial properties of C-QDs modified TiO2 as a photocatalyst for MB degradation in visible region. The fabricated TiO2 photocatalyst degraded 90% MB dye in 2 hours [28]. Moreover, most of the as-prepared materials are toxic and need special detoxification techniques under UV-visible photocatalysis. Another associated problem is their separation efficiency from reaction medium for reusability [29–31].

The present research offers a versatile way to fabricate non-toxic C-QDs encapsulated with silica. Silica has porous surface area which assists C-QDs encapsulation and surface passivation thus increasing thier catalytic efficiency [32–35]. Consequently, the repeated results showed that the as-prepared materials can act as efficient photocatalysts for MB degradation and can be efficiently used for three consecutive cycles. The novelty of this work involves the use of non-toxic silica as surface passivating agents, subsequently, the surface area of carbon dots/silica increases many folds for efficient degradation of organic dye MB.

All the chemicals are purchased from commercial sources and were used without further purification. Ethanol (99.50%, Sigma-Aldrich), Water (De-ionized), Hydrochloric acid (37%, Sigma-Aldrich), Nitric acid (65%, Sigma-Aldrich), Potassium hydroxide (≥85%, Sigma-Aldrich), Tetraethyl orthosilicate (98%, Sigma-Aldrich), Glucose-D (≥99%, Sigma-Aldrich), Sulphuric acid (98%, Sigma-Aldrich), Acetone (≥99%, Sigma-Aldrich), Chloroform (≥99%, Sigma-Aldrich) and Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB, 98%, Sigma-Aldrich) were used as received.

Silica was prepared by Sol-gel method as reported elsewhere with slight modifications (Scheme 1) [36]. Briefly, three drops of HCl (37%) were added to a constantly stirring mixture (25 mL) of water and ethanol (1:4) at 60°C for 20 mins, followed by dropwise addition of 2 mL of Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS). The reaction mixture was then continuously stirred at 200 rpm for the next 180 mins at 60°C to hydrolyze TEOS completely. Once TEOS was hydrolyzed, white color solid appeared in the flask. Upon completion, the as-synthesized silica was centrifuged, washed several times with ethanol, and dried in an oven at 70°C for 60 mins.

C-QDs were prepared by dehydration of glucose-D (Scheme 2). Hence, concentrated sulphuric acid (4 mL) was slowly added to Glucose-D (0.50 gm) under constant stirring at 300 rpm for 20 mins at room temperature. After 20 mins, the reaction mixture turned black indicating the synthesis of C-QDs. Later, the as-synthesized C-QDs were extracted using a solvent mixture of water, chloroform, and ethanol (1:1:3). The extracted C-QDs were sonicated for 30 mins to separate them from the bulk material and purified by centrifugation at 3000 rpm for 60 mins. The solvent mixture was removed through rotary evaporation to obtain the purified C-QDs [37].

The preparation of C-QDs doped silica was performed in situ by adding the as-prepared C-QDs in silica which serve as a scaffold [38]. The as-prepared C-QDs (2 mL) were dissolved in a 25 mL mixture of water and ethanol (1:4), followed by the addition of HCl (3-drops) under constant stirring of 300 rpm at room temperature. After 20 mins of stirring, the precursor TEOS (2 mL) was added dropwise to the reaction mixture within 2 mins. The temperature of the reaction mixture was gradually raised up to 60°C with constant stirring. After the required time, the reaction mixture was stirred for further 180 mins at 60°C to hydrolyze TEOS completely. Once the reaction was completed, brown-colored solid precipitates of the prepared doped silica appeared slowly in the reaction mixture which were filtered, centrifuged, washed with ethanol, and dried in an oven at 70°C for 60 mins.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of C-QDs doped calcined silica
ce:section.ce:para: C-QDs doped calcined silica was prepared as reported but with minor modifications. Hence, HCl (3 drops) were added to 25 mL mixture of water and ethanol (1:4), followed by CTAB (0.50 gm) under stirring (300 rpm) at room temperature. Once CTAB was dissolved, TEOS (2 mL) was added dropwise to the reaction and mixture was stirred for 180 mins at 60°C to hydrolyze TEOS completely. The synthesized silica was then centrifuged, washed with ethanol, and dried in oven at 60°C for 60 mins. Silica was then calcined at 800°C for 180 mins in a microprocessor-controlled muffle furnace at 10°C/mins. Then encapsulation of C-QDs was achieved by dissolving 2 mL of C-QDs in 25 ml mixture of water and ethanol (1:4). Later, HCl (3 drops) were added to the solvents mixture, followed by addition of calcined silica under stirring of 300 rpm 60°C for 180 mins. The synthesized calcined doped silica was centrifuged, washed with ethanol, and dried at room temperature [37–39].

The as-prepared photocatalysts (0.80 gm) were added to the MB dye solution (6.5 × 10−6 M), placed in dark for 20 mins to establish adsorption equilibrium, and then zero-time response was recorded. The time-dependent % degradation was analyzed via the absorption spectrum of MB under UV irradiation. About 5 mL of the solution was taken from the reaction mixture and centrifuged for 5 mins to remove the coagulated photocatalysts. Using Cecil UV-visible spectrophotometer (200-1100 nm), the absorption spectrum of the dye was acquired as a function of irradiation time. The rate of decolorization was observed from the absorption spectra in terms of change in intensity at λmax=667 nm and the % degradation was calculated using equation (1) given below [1].
Degradation
( % )
=
(
Ao − At
Ao
)
×
100
Ao=Absorbance at time 0. At=Absorbance at time t.

p-XRD was used to characterize the as-prepared C-QDs. Sharp peak with low intensity counts at 2θ=23°, 43° and 47° corresponds to (002) plane of graphite (d002 =3.6 A°) and 101 plane originated from in plane diffractions of graphene like structures fig.1 (a). The absence of no other peaks indicates that as-prepared C-QDs are amorphous in nature with little crystallinity and similar results have been reported in literature [40–42]. p-XRD results of the as-prepared silica (b), doped silica (c), and calcined doped silica (d) show broad peak with low-intensity counts at 2θ=230 consistent to an interplanar spacing of 3.55 Å which is larger than the spacing between (002) planes in bulk. The results show very short-order Si-O-Si structure and confirm their amorphous nature fig.1(b-d).

UV-Vis spectrum clearly depicts absorption of C-QDs in the UV region at 270 nm with extended tail in the visible region. The resultant sharp peak at 270 cm−1 represents
π − π
* and n-π* transitions indicating polycyclic conjugated aromatic core skeleton and the surface group fig. 2 [10,43,44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Field Emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) analysis
ce:section.ce:para: FE-SEM analysis determined morphological aspects of the as-prepared materials fig.3 (a-d). Accordingly, plain silica has spherical grains like with smooth surfaces fig. 3(a-b). After incorporation of C-QDs, their morphology changed from spherical to fused with rough surfaces fig. 3(c-d) [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparative photocatalytic degradation of MB dye
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic experiment was performed using fixed contents of MB dye and photocatalysts. Degradation obtained for C-QDs was 38% after 119 mins fig. 4 (a). Whereas with bare silica and doped silica, degradation was found to be 76% fig. 4(b) and 92.50% fig. 4(c) after 44 mins. As mentioned earlier, silica acts as a capping source, absorbs C-QDs over its surface and exposes its active sites for efficient degradation. The surface excitation of C-QDs leads to redox reaction which results in degradation of MB dye [21 32,46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of changing concentration of photocatalysts and MB dye
ce:section.ce:para: fig. 5 (a) shows the effect of C-QDs concentration on the photocatalysis of doped silica. The degradation observed was 95% within no time. With increase in the concentration of C-QDs, active surface area of the photocatalysts increases which results in improved photodegradation of MB dye. Moreover, fig. 5(b) shows the photocatalytic activity of doped silica using different concentrations of MB dye (92.50% in 44 mins, 99% in 49 mins). It was observed that with the dilution of the dye solution, the rate of degradation increases. Hence upon dilution, the chances for the dye to get adsorbed over the surface of silica for active degradation increases [46–48].

Photocatalytic degradation was observed at different pH values using doped silica. In basic medium (pH=12), the rate of degradation was well above 98% within no time fig. 6 (b), whereas in acidic medium (pH=02), decrease in degradation was found and only 82% degradation occurred after 119 mins fig. 6(a). The degradation results at neutral pH were found to be intermediate (92%) i.e higher than acidic medium and lower than basic medium fig. 4(c). The maximum degradation observed in basic medium was mainly due to increased OH− concentration. Conversely, in acidic medium the concentration of H+ increases, the availability of OH− ions decreases hence fewer radicals are produced to degrade MB dye [49–52].

Calcination imparts silica the ability to absorb dye particles efficiently hence enhances degradation. Consequently, the calcined materials displayed improved degradation of MB dye. Hence, the activity of doped silica was found to be the highest (98.50%, 49 mins) fig. 7 (a) than the uncalcined doped silica (92.50%, 44 mins) given in fig. 4(c).

Reproducibility of the as-prepared photocatalysts was studied experimentally. The photocatalytic results depict that the calcined doped silica has higher reproducibility. The doped calcined photocatalytic system was found to be highly reproducible for three consecutive cycles (98.50% in 49 mins, 97% in 119 mins, 92.60% in 119 mins) shown in figs. 7, 8
(b-c) [32,43,52].

The present study reports the efficient preparation, characterization of C-QDs encapsulated with silica through Sol-gel process. h The as-prepared C-QDs were used to remove toxic dye MB from water. The results obtained clearly indicate that silica encapsulated C-QDs are more efficient than the pristine silica which in turn has higher degradation ability than C-QDs. Similarly, the silica encapsulated CQDs was found to be more efficient in basic than acidic and neutral media. They also showed best photocatalytic activity with the low dilution of the MB dye solution. Further more, calcinated silica encapsulated C-QDs also showed better catalytic performance presmubly due to higher surface area. The development of such heterogeneous photocatalytic systems could pave the way towards realization of sustainable green catalysis, and it can be further extended to organic synthesis as-well.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Dr. Hamid Aziz acknowledges support of the Higher Education Commission (HEC), Pakistan, by awarding indigenous scholarship
